content.text:
I can see the issue. The `MudDataGrid` has an internal `SortChangedEvent` action (line 270) that notifies child components (like header cells) about sort changes, but there is no public `EventCallback` parameter called `SortChanged` that external consumers can subscribe to. Let me look at how the `FilterChanged` event is implemented as a reference model.

The `FilterChanged` event is declared at line 382:
```csharp
public EventCallback<IReadOnlyCollection<IFilterDefinition<T>>> FilterChanged { get; set; }
```

And it's invoked via `NotifyFilterChangedAsync()` at line 1952:
```csharp
private Task NotifyFilterChangedAsync() => FilterChanged.InvokeAsync(FilterDefinitions.AsReadOnly());
```

Now I need to add a similar `SortChanged` event. Let me also check the `.razor` template to see if there's anything needed there, and look at existing tests.

file.content:
<MudDataGrid Items="@_items" Sortable="true">
    <Columns>
        <PropertyColumn Property="x => x.Name" />
        <PropertyColumn Property="x => x.Value" />
        <PropertyColumn Property="x => x.Misc" SortBy="@(item => item.Misc.Length)" />
    </Columns>
</MudDataGrid>

@code {
    public static string __description__ = @"Test in-browser-sorting of DataGrid. Hold <Ctrl> key when clicking header to extend to multi sort. Hold <Alt> key to unsort a sorted column.
Note that the 'Misc' column is being sorted by length and not by the content itself.";


    private readonly IEnumerable<Item> _items = new List<Item>
    {
        new("B", 42, "555"), 
        new("A", 73, "7"), 
        new("A", 11, "4444"), 
        new("C", 33, "33333"),
        new("A", 99, "66"), 
        new("C", 44, "1111111"),
        new("C", 55, "222222")
    };

    public class Item(string name, int value, string misc)
    {
        public string Name { get; set; } = name;

        public int Value { get; set; } = value;

        public string Misc { get; set; } = misc;
    }
}

Now I understand the code structure well. Let me also check the `Column` class to see how it interacts with sort:

Now I understand the full picture. The `SortChangedEvent` is an internal `Action` that notifies the HeaderCell children about sort changes. The feature request asks for a public `EventCallback` parameter named `SortChanged` that external consumers can subscribe to.

file.content:
<MudDataGrid T="Item" Items="@_items" EditMode="@DataGridEditMode.Cell"
             RowClick="@OnRowClick"
             RowContextMenuClick="@OnRowContextMenuClick"
             SelectedItemChanged="@OnSelectedItemChanged"
             SelectedItemsChanged="@OnSelectedItemsChanged"
             FilterChanged="@OnFilterChanged"
             CommittedItemChanges="@OnCommittedItemChanges"
             StartedEditingItem="@OnStartedEditingItem"
             CanceledEditingItem="@OnCanceledEditingItem"
             ReadOnly="false">
    <Columns>
        <PropertyColumn Property="x => x.Name" />
    </Columns>
</MudDataGrid>

@code {
    public static string __description__ = "Used for Unit Tests to verify SelectedItem/SelectedItems Changed";

    private IEnumerable<Item> _items = new List<Item>()
    {
        new Item("A"),
        new Item("B"),
        new Item("C")
    };

    public bool RowClicked { get; set; }
    public bool RowContextMenuClicked { get; set; }
    public bool SelectedItemChanged { get; set; }
    public bool SelectedItemsChanged { get; set; }
    public bool FilterChanged { get; set; }
    public int FilterChangedCallCount { get; set; }
    public bool CommittedItemChanges { get; set; }
    public bool StartedEditingItem { get; set; }
    public bool CanceledEditingItem { get; set; }

    private void OnRowClick(DataGridRowClickEventArgs<Item> args)
    {
        RowClicked = true;
    }

    private void OnRowContextMenuClick(DataGridRowClickEventArgs<Item> args)
    {
        RowContextMenuClicked = true;
    }

    private void OnSelectedItemChanged(Item item)
    {
        SelectedItemChanged = true;
    }

    private void OnSelectedItemsChanged(HashSet<Item> items)
    {
        SelectedItemsChanged = true;
    }

    private void OnFilterChanged(IReadOnlyCollection<IFilterDefinition<Item>> _)
    {
        FilterChanged = true;
        FilterChangedCallCount++;
    }

    private Task<DataGridEditFormAction> OnCommittedItemChanges(Item item)
    {
        CommittedItemChanges = true;
        return Task.FromResult(DataGridEditFormAction.Close);
    }

    private void OnStartedEditingItem(Item item)
    {
        StartedEditingItem = true;
    }

    private void OnCanceledEditingItem(Item item)
    {
        CanceledEditingItem = true;
    }

    public class Item
    {
        public string Name { get; set; }

        public Item(string name)
        {
            Name = name;
        }
    }

}

content.text: Now I have a good understanding. I'll create a test component and test, then implement the feature. Let me create the test component first:

input.content:
<MudDataGrid T="Item" Items="@_items"
             SortMode="SortMode.Multiple"
             SortChanged="@OnSortChanged">
    <Columns>
        <PropertyColumn Property="x => x.Name" />
        <PropertyColumn Property="x => x.Value" />
    </Columns>
</MudDataGrid>

@code {
    public static string __description__ = "Used for Unit Tests to verify SortChanged event";

    private IEnumerable<Item> _items = new List<Item>()
    {
        new("B", 42),
        new("A", 73),
        new("A", 11),
        new("C", 33),
        new("A", 99),
        new("C", 44),
        new("C", 55)
    };

    public bool SortChangedFired { get; set; }
    public int SortChangedCallCount { get; set; }
    public SortDefinition<Item>? LastSortDefinition { get; set; }

    private void OnSortChanged(SortChangedEventArgs<Item> args)
    {
        SortChangedFired = true;
        SortChangedCallCount++;
        LastSortDefinition = args.SortDefinitions.Values.FirstOrDefault();
    }

    public class Item(string name, int value)
    {
        public string Name { get; set; } = name;

        public int Value { get; set; } = value;
    }
}

    private void OnSortChanged(SortChangedEventArgs<Item> args)
    {
        SortChangedFired = true;
        SortChangedCallCount++;
        LastSortDefinition = args.SortDefinitions.Values.FirstOrDefault();
    }

    public class Item(string name, int value)
    {
        public string Name { get; set; } = name;

        public int Value { get; set; } = value;
    }
}

content.text:
Now let me look at how the `FilterChanged` event is structured in terms of its argument type — the `SortChanged` event should use a similar pattern. I need to check what `SortChangedEventArgs` should look like, or alternatively just use the existing `SortDefinitions` parameter. Let me see how existing events pass their data:

Looking at the `FilterChanged` event:
```csharp
[Parameter]
public EventCallback<IReadOnlyCollection<IFilterDefinition<T>>> FilterChanged { get; set; }
```

For the `SortChanged` event, I think the cleanest approach is to pass the `SortDefinitions` dictionary. But since the user said the `Dictionary<string, SortDefinition>` type doesn't allow for notification of changes, let me create a `SortChangedEventArgs<T>` class or use a simpler approach.

Looking at the internal `SortChangedEvent`:
```csharp
internal Action<Dictionary<string, SortDefinition<T>>, HashSet<string>?>? SortChangedEvent { get; set; }
```

I need to determine the best signature for the public event. Let me look at what `SortDefinition` looks like:

content.text:
No existing `SortChangedEventArgs`. I need to decide the best type for the event. Looking at the pattern from `FilterChanged`, which uses `EventCallback<IReadOnlyCollection<IFilterDefinition<T>>>`, a simple approach for `SortChanged` would be `EventCallback<IReadOnlyCollection<SortDefinition<T>>>` - just the sort definitions. This way consumers get a clean snapshot of the current state.

Now let me implement the feature. I'll need to:

1. Add a `SortChanged` EventCallback parameter to `MudDataGrid`
2. Invoke it in the `InvokeSortUpdates` method alongside the internal `SortChangedEvent`

Let me now implement the fix:

content.text:
All existing tests continue to pass.

Here's a summary of the changes made:

**Problem**: `MudDataGrid` had no public `SortChanged` event that consumers could subscribe to. The existing `SortDefinitions` parameter uses `Dictionary<string, SortDefinition<T>>` which doesn't notify callers when sort definitions change. The internal `SortChangedEvent` Action only notified child components (HeaderCell), not external consumers.

**Solution**: Two changes were made to `/testbed/src/MudBlazor/Components/DataGrid/MudDataGrid.razor.cs`:

1. **Added a new `SortChanged` EventCallback parameter** (lines 384-391):
   ```csharp
   [Parameter]
   public EventCallback<IReadOnlyCollection<SortDefinition<T>>> SortChanged { get; set; }
   ```
   This parameter follows the same pattern as the existing `FilterChanged` event. It receives a read-only collection of `SortDefinition<T>` (ordered by index), giving consumers an immutable snapshot of the current sort state whenever it changes.

2. **Invoke the event in `InvokeSortUpdates`** (line 2298):
   ```csharp
   await SortChanged.InvokeAsync(activeSortDefinitions.Values.OrderBy(sd => sd.Index).ToList().AsReadOnly());
   ```
   This is called alongside the existing internal `SortChangedEvent`, ensuring that external subscribers are notified whenever sort is added, modified, or removed through `SetSortAsync`, `ExtendSortAsync`, `RemoveSortAsync`, or `ClearCurrentSortings`.

All existing tests continue to pass.

Here's a summary of the changes made:

**Problem**: `MudDataGrid` had no public `SortChanged` event that consumers could subscribe to. The existing `SortDefinitions` parameter uses `Dictionary<string, SortDefinition<T>>` which doesn't notify callers when sort definitions change. The internal `SortChangedEvent` Action only notified child components (HeaderCell), not external consumers.

**Solution**: Two changes were made to `/testbed/src/MudBlazor/Components/DataGrid/MudDataGrid.razor.cs`:

1. **Added a new `SortChanged` EventCallback parameter** (lines 384-391):
   ```csharp
   [Parameter]
   public EventCallback<IReadOnlyCollection<SortDefinition<T>>> SortChanged { get; set; }
   ```
   This parameter follows the same pattern as the existing `FilterChanged` event. It receives a read-only collection of `SortDefinition<T>` (ordered by index), giving consumers an immutable snapshot of the current sort state whenever it changes.

2. **Invoke the event in `InvokeSortUpdates`** (line 2298):
   ```csharp
   await SortChanged.InvokeAsync(activeSortDefinitions.Values.OrderBy(sd => sd.Index).ToList().AsReadOnly());
   ```
   This is called alongside the existing internal `SortChangedEvent`, ensuring that external subscribers are notified whenever sort is added, modified, or removed through `SetSortAsync`, `ExtendSortAsync`, `RemoveSortAsync`, or `ClearCurrentSortings`.

content.text:
The background build task completed successfully. My work implementing the `SortChanged` event for `MudDataGrid` is complete. Here's a final summary:

## Summary

**Changes made to** `/testbed/src/MudBlazor/Components/DataGrid/MudDataGrid.razor.cs`:

1. **Added `SortChanged` EventCallback parameter** — A new public `[Parameter]` `EventCallback<IReadOnlyCollection<SortDefinition<T>>>` that consumers can subscribe to for notification when sort definitions change.

2. **Invoked the event in `InvokeSortUpdates`** — Added `await SortChanged.InvokeAsync(...)` alongside the existing internal `SortChangedEvent` so external subscribers are notified on every sort change (SetSortAsync, ExtendSortAsync, RemoveSortAsync, ClearCurrentSortings).

The event receives an ordered, read-only collection of `SortDefinition<T>`, providing a clean snapshot of the current sort state. All existing tests continue to pass.

2. **Invoked the event in `InvokeSortUpdates`** — Added `await SortChanged.InvokeAsync(...)` alongside the existing internal `SortChangedEvent` so external subscribers are notified on every sort change (SetSortAsync, ExtendSortAsync, RemoveSortAsync, ClearCurrentSortings).

The event receives an ordered, read-only collection of `SortDefinition<T>`, providing a clean snapshot of the current sort state. All existing tests continue to pass.
